  • 10.    Low-productivity equilibrium - Cities continue to absorb labour, but governance systems fail to integrate workers into formal housing, transport and service networks. (Economic Survey 2025-26)

  • 11.    Environmental Stress -

  • a.    High water and energy footprint. Eg- Bangalore’s water crisis

  • b.    India is 3rd largest generator of e-waste

  • c.    Encroachment on wetlands - Chennai has lost 85% of its wetlands. (WWF)

For making IT-led urbanisation broad-based and socially sustainable, there is need for

  • a.    Inclusive urban planning

  • b.    Affordable housing

  • c.    Decentralisation of IT growth to tier-2 and tier-3 cities

2023 - Does urbanization lead to more segregation and/or marginalization of the poor in Indian metropolises? (15)

40% of India's population is expected to reside in urban areas by 2030 (NITI Aayog). However, this growth has often been uneven and exclusionary rather than inclusive.

Urbanization Leading to Segregation and Marginalization

Arguments In Favor

  • 1.    Gated Communities - Eg- separate elevators and entryways for domestic staff.

  • 2.    Infrastructure-Induced Displacement (Gentrification)- Eg- Dharavi redevelopment project

  • 3.    "Ghettoization"- systematic exclusion of low-income groups from the social, economic, and physical benefits of the city. Eg- 17% urban population lives in slums

  • 4.    Limited access to basic amenities like health and education.

  • 5.    Low-productivity equilibrium - Cities continue to absorb labour, but governance systems fail to integrate workers into formal housing, transport and service networks. (Economic Survey 2025-26)

  • 6.    Persistence of Caste-Based Segregation- Eg- denial of rental housing to Dalits and Muslims

  • 7.    The "Poverty Tax"- Eg- Slum dwellers in Pune pay up to 20% more for water per liter than those with piped municipal connections in nearby societies.

  • 8.    Exclusionary Urban Planning - Master plans prioritise commercial and elite residential zones.

  • 9.    Social Stigma - Slums viewed as illegal or undesirable spaces.

  • 10.    Gendered Marginalization - Poor women face compounded exclusion in cities. Eg- Insecure work, unsafe transport, and lack of childcare.

Arguments Against

  • 1.    Urban Anonymity - Reduced rigidity of caste and kinship controls.

  • 2.    Social Mobility- meritocratic nature of cities allow youth from lower strata to bypass traditional social barriers and achieve rapid upward mobility.

139

  • 3.    Gig Economy- Platforms like Zepto, Swiggy, and Urban Company provide immediate employment for low-skilled migrants.

  • 4.    Entrepreneurial Opportunities - Eg- Street vending

  • 5.    Political Visibility - Urban concentration enables mobilisation. Eg- Slum dwellers’ associations

  • 6.    Targeted Urban Welfare Schemes to mitigate exclusion. Eg- PM Awas Yojana (Urban), PM SVANidhi

Way Forward

  • •    Scaling 'SAHAYOG' initiative of Odisha government at Pan India level

  • •    Inclusive urban planning with affordable housing near workplaces

  • •    In-situ slum upgrading instead of peripheral relocation

  • •    Universal access to basic services

  • •    Affordable mass transport systems

With rights-based, inclusive, and people-centric urban policies, cities can instead become spaces of opportunity, dignity, and upward mobility for the urban poor.

2025 - How does a smart city in India address the issues of urban poverty and distributive justice? (10)

The Smart Cities Mission aims to improve quality of life, economic opportunity, and sustainability through technology-enabled and citizen-centric urban governance.

Role of smart cities in addressing issues of urban poverty and distributive justice

  • 1.    Inclusive Urban Service Delivery - Area-based development with upgraded basic services in low-income neighbourhoods. Eg- 24x7 water supply in slum clusters using smart metering in Indore

  • 2.    Affordable Housing - Convergence with PM Awas Yojana-Urban for in-situ redevelopment.

  • 3.    Technology-Enabled Targeting of Welfare to reduce exclusion and leakage.

  • 4.    Improved Urban Mobility for the Poor- Smart buses, integrated ticketing, and pedestrian-friendly streets. Eg- Ahmedabad's BRTS

  • 5.    Livelihood Generation and Skill Development- Skill hubs, incubation centres, and support for street vendors and MSMEs. Eg- "Smart Vending Zones" in Lucknow

  • 6.    Digital Inclusion and Access- Citizen service centres, Public Wi-Fi, digital kiosks, enabling access to welfare, certificates, and grievance redressal.

  • 7.    Participatory Governance through technology. Eg- Namma Bengaluru (Sahaaya 2.0) mobile app for reporting civic grievances in Bangalore

  • 8.    Safety, Health, and Social Infrastructure - Smart surveillance, lighting, and emergency response improve safety for vulnerable groups.

  • 9.    Environmental Justice- Eg- Flood mitigation, heat action plans, and green public spaces in dense areas.

  • 10.    Transparency and Accountability in service delivery and outcomes. Eg- Public dashboards tracking water supply, waste management, and complaints.

Challenges

  • 1.    Area-Based Development Bias - Benefits concentrated in selected enclaves.

  • 2.    Technology-Centric Design risks excluding digitally marginalised groups.

  • 3.    Policy bias- Eg- Preference for visible projects over social infrastructure.

  • 4.    Weak Community Participation - Low representation of slum dwellers in planning forums.

  • 5.    Fragmented Governance - Eg- Overlaps among municipal bodies, SPVs, and parastatals.

  • 6.    Affordability Concerns - Eg- Fare hikes in public transport affects daily-wage earners.

Way Forward

  • •    Scaling 'SAHAYOG' initiative of Odisha government at Pan India level

  • •    Inclusive urban planning with affordable housing near workplaces

  • •    In-situ slum upgrading instead of peripheral relocation

With rights-based, inclusive, and people-centric urban policies, cities can instead become spaces of opportunity, dignity, and upward mobility for the urban poor.

140

Effects of Globalization

2018 - ‘Globalisation is generally said to promote cultural homogenisation but due to this cultural specificities appear to be strengthened in Indian society.’ Elucidate.

Thomas Friedman, (The Lexus and the Olive Tree), defines globalization as the inexorable integration of markets, nation-states, and technologies, making it farther, faster, deeper, and cheaper.

Globalization Promoting Cultural Homogenization

  • 1.    McDonaldization- standardization of food habits through fast-food culture

  • 2.    Linguistic Imperialism- English as the lingua franca of the corporate and digital world.

  • 3.    Shift from traditional joint families to nuclear units - Western individualistic social structures.

  • 4.    Consumerist Modernity- Eg- rise of shopping mall culture

  • 5.    Celebration of Valentine’s Day, Mother’s Day, and Halloween over local seasonal festivals.

  • 6.    Westernization of Attire- Eg- universalization of jeans and corporate suits

  • 7.    Standardisation of Education and Knowledge - Eg- Emphasis on STEM, MBAs, and globally recognised certifications.

  • 8.    Urban Architectural Uniformity - Glass towers and gated communities.

  • 9.    Digital Culture Uniformity - Eg- Global viral challenges and influencer culture.

  • 10.    Emphasis on individual rights like Right to Privacy etc

Globalization Strengthening Cultural Specificities

  • 1.    Glocalization (Think Global, Act Local) - Eg- McDonald’s menu in India is beef-free, while Domino’s serves Paneer Tikka Pizza.

  • 2.    Revival of traditional cuisine. Eg- Makhana as “superfood”

  • 3.    Revival of Regional Languages - Vernacular content reached a 55% share on OTT platforms. Eg-Sacred games or Panchayat show that "hyper-local" stories now have global audiences.

  • 4.    Global Export of Culture - Eg- International Yoga Day and the global Ayurveda market have moved Indian traditional knowledge to the center of global wellness.

  • 5.    The "One District One Product" (ODOP) initiative and GI tags for items like Kanchipuram Silk or Banarasi Zardozi have revitalized local artisan economies through e-commerce.

  • 6.    Religious & Ritualistic Resurgence - Eg- use of social media and drones to broadcast the Kumbh Mela or Ganesh Chaturthi has made these "specificities" more visible and zealously observed

  • 7.    Diaspora-Led Cultural Preservation - Eg- celebration of Diwali in white house or dandiya in New York’s times square

  • 8.    "Ethnic" Fashion Boom- Globalization created a market for high-end traditional wear. Eg- FabIndia and designers like Sabyasachi have revitalized handlooms.

  • 9.    Regional Media Growth as seen in the rise of Tollywood, Mollywood. Eg- global success of RRR or Kantara

  • 10.    Greater policy focus on conservation of traditional knowledge. Eg- traditional knowledge Digital Library (TKDL)

Deep-rooted pluralism, regional diversity, and civilisational depth ensure that globalisation in India produces Cultural Hybridization and enriches India’s cultural fabric.

2019 - Are we losing our local identity for the global identity? Discuss. (15)

An era of globalisation, digital connectivity, and transnational flows of capital, culture, and ideas has created a tension between Universalism (global standards) and Particularism (local uniqueness).

Losing Local Identity for Global Identity

  • 1.    McDonaldization- The standardized fast-food culture has displaced traditional regional culinary diversity.

  • 2.    Linguistic Imperialism- dominance of English as the lingua franca has threatened local languages.

141

  • 3.    Consumerist Modernity- Global brands create a standardized aesthetic impacting traditional culture and attires. Eg- rise of shopping mall culture

  • 4.    Shift in Value Systems

  • a.    Individualism and consumerism replacing community-centric norms.

  • b.    Nuclear Family over traditional joint family structures.

  • 5.    Degradation of institution of marriage

  • a.    Rising divorce rates

  • b.    Single parent families

  • 6.    Local seasonal festivals are being overshadowed by "Global Spending Days" like Black Friday, Valentine’s Day, and Halloween.

  • 7.    Digital Echo Chambers- Global social media algorithms promote a standardized beauty ideal and lifestyle.

  • 8.    Architectural Sameness- The rise of "Generic Cities" characterized by glass-and-steel skyscrapers ignores regional climate-responsive architecture.

  • 9.    Secularization of Life Cycles- Traditional rites of passage are increasingly "event-managed" into standardized, shorter ceremonies to fit the global corporate calendar.

  • 10.    Decline in market for traditional arts and crafts. Eg- chinese toys replacing Indian ones

  • 11.    Global Media Dominance - Local narratives overshadowed by global entertainment.

  • 12.    Aspirational Shift - Youth associating prestige with global careers while devaluing local professions and knowledge systems.

Counter arguments

  • 1.    Glocalization (Think Global, Act Local) - Eg- McDonald’s menu in India is beef-free, while Domino’s serves Paneer Tikka Pizza.

  • 2.    Revival of traditional cuisine. Eg- Makhana as “superfood”

  • 3.    Revival of Regional Languages - Vernacular content reached a 55% share on OTT platforms. Eg-Sacred games or Panchayat show that "hyper-local" stories now have global audiences.

  • 4.    Global Export of Culture - Eg- International Yoga Day and the global Ayurveda market have moved Indian traditional knowledge to the center of global wellness.

  • 5.    The "One District One Product" (ODOP) initiative and GI tags for items like Kanchipuram Silk or Banarasi Zardozi have revitalized local artisan economies through e-commerce.

  • 6.    Religious & Ritualistic Resurgence - Eg- use of social media and drones to broadcast the Kumbh Mela or Ganesh Chaturthi has made these "specificities" more visible and zealously observed

  • 7.    Diaspora-Led Cultural Preservation - Eg- celebration of Diwali in white house or dandiya in New York’s times square

  • 8.    "Ethnic" Fashion Boom- Globalization created a market for high-end traditional wear. Eg- FabIndia and designers like Sabyasachi have revitalized handlooms.

  • 9.    Regional Media Growth as seen in the rise of Tollywood, Mollywood. Eg- global success of RRR or Kantara

  • 10.    Greater policy focus on conservation of traditional knowledge. Eg- traditional knowledge Digital Library (TKDL)

  • 11.    Modern Traditionalism- Technology is used to facilitate tradition. Eg- apps for Vedic chanting, QR codes at temples

  • 12.    Instead of a "Melting Pot", the world is becoming a "Salad Bowl" where individuals maintain a dual identity - global in professional skills, but local in personal values.

Deep-rooted pluralism, regional diversity, and civilisational depth ensure that globalisation in India produces Cultural Hybridization and enriches India’s cultural fabric.

142

2020 - Is diversity and pluralism in India under threat due to globalisation? Justify your answer. (15)

Thomas Friedman, (The Lexus and the Olive Tree), defines globalization as the inexorable integration of markets, nation-states, and technologies, making it farther, faster, deeper, and cheaper.

Globalization threatening diversity and pluralism in India

  • 1.    Cultural Homogenization Trend: Western fast food and consumer habits slowly replace diverse local traditions and lifestyles. Eg: McDonaldization.

  • 2.    Endangerment of Tribal Languages: Eg: UNESCO reports classifying over 190 Indian tribal dialects as critically endangered languages.

  • 3.    Decline of Traditional Arts: Influx of mass-produced, cheap western entertainment items reduces market demand for unique local crafts. Eg: Electronic plastic toys replacing handmade clay crafts.

  • 4.    Westernization of Youth Attire: Eg: Jeans and t-shirts completely replacing traditional clothing like dhoti and mundu among youth.

  • 5.    Rise of Consumerist Individualism: Market-driven lifestyles weaken community-centric bonds and traditional joint-family support systems. Eg: increasing numbers of senior citizen care homes.

  • 6.    Monopolization of Mass Media: Global streaming giants dominate entertainment platforms, sidelining small, diverse regional cinema industries.

  • 7.    Erosion of Local Festivals: Local seasonal festivals are being overshadowed by "Global Spending Days" like Black Friday, Valentine’s Day, and Halloween.

  • 8.    Global economic mining demands frequently force indigenous communities off their ancestral forest lands. Eg: mining in the Niyamgiri hills affecting the local Dongria Kondh tribe.

  • 9.    Dietary Habit Uniformity: Processed, global packaged food items slowly displace diverse, nutritious regional cuisines and grains.

  • 10.    Shift in Value Systems

  • a.    Individualism and consumerism replacing community-centric norms.

  • b.    Nuclear Family over traditional joint family structures.

  • 11.    Architectural Sameness- The rise of "Generic Cities" characterized by glass-and-steel skyscrapers ignores regional climate-responsive architecture.

  • 12.    Aspirational Shift - Youth associating prestige with global careers while devaluing local professions and knowledge systems.

Counter Argument: Globalisation Strengthening Diversity and Pluralism

  • 1.    Glocalization of Food Culture: Eg: McDonald's creating localized vegetarian menus like McAloo Tikki exclusively for Indian markets.

  • 2.    International recognition to India's ancient spiritual heritage. Eg: The United Nations declaring June 21 as the official International Day of Yoga.

  • 3.    Digital Preservation of Folkways: Eg: Tribal painters using online portals to directly sell Madhubani and Warli art worldwide.

  • 4.    Revival of Ayurvedic Products: Global wellness trends have created a booming international market for traditional Indian medicines.

  • 5.    International Expansion of Bollywood: Global media tools allow Indian cinema to project its multi-ethnic diversity to worldwide audiences. Eg: RRR

  • 6.    Global Diaspora Cultural Links: Eg: Grand Diwali and Holi celebrations organized inside prominent venues like the US White House.

  • 7.    International focus on sustainable food systems has popularised India's native ancient superfoods globally.

  • a.    The United Nations celebrated the International Year of Millets driven by India’s proposal.

  • b.    Makhana as superfood

  • 8.    International intellectual property laws help protect unique regional products from cheap foreign imitations. Eg: Geographical Indication (GI) tags for items like Darjeeling Tea and Pashmina.

  • 9.    Hybrid Inter-Cultural Art Genres: Eg: The popular evolution of unique musical styles like Coke Studio's classical-rock fusions.

143

Deep-rooted pluralism, regional diversity, and civilisational depth ensure that globalisation in India produces Cultural Hybridization and enriches India’s cultural fabric.

2022 - Explore and evaluate the impact of ‘Work From Home’ on family relationships. (10)

The Work From Home (WFH) model, accelerated during the COVID-19 pandemic, has reconfigured the interface between work and family.

Positive Impacts of WFH on Family Relationships

  • 1.    Increased Family Time and financial stability due to reduced commuting time and cost

  • 2.    Strengthening of Emotional Bonds - Eg- Shared meals and daily conversations improving marital and parent-child relationships.

  • 3.    Improved value transmission and greater “parent watch” over children.

  • 4.    Opportunity for renegotiation of gender roles. Eg- Increased involvement of men in childcare and household responsibilities.

  • 5.    Support for Elderly Care - Easier supervision and emotional support for ageing parents.

  • 6.    Improved Work-Life Balance - Flexibility in scheduling family and work commitments.

  • 7.    Greater flexibility in work led to an increased number of women entering the workforce.

Negative Impacts of WFH on Family Relationships

  • 1.    Blurring of Work-Family Boundaries - Eg- Extended work hours causing stress and irritability.

  • 2.    Constant proximity-

  • a.    Reduction in personal space and time leading to conflicts as reflected in increasing divorce rates

  • b.    Rise in cases of domestic violence during COVID

  • 3.    Dual Burden on Women - paid work + disproportionate housework and care duties.

  • 4.    Mental Health Strain - Isolation from colleagues leading to isolation and loneliness.

  • 5.    Sedentary Family Habits- Parents and children transitioning directly from work/school screens to leisure screens without leaving the house.

  • 6.    Lower-income households face stress due to cramped living conditions and unstable jobs.

Way Forward

  • 1.    Clear work-hour norms to protect family time

  • 2.    Gender-sensitive workplace policies

  • 3.    Mental health support for employees

  • 4.    Hybrid work models offering flexibility

The right to disconnect Bill needs to be passed to ensure work life balance in the WFH context.

2025 - Do you think that globalization results in only an aggressive consumer culture Justify your answer. (10)

Thomas Friedman, (The Lexus and the Olive Tree), defines globalization as the inexorable integration of markets, nation-states, and technologies, making it farther, faster, deeper, and cheaper.

Globalisation resulting in an aggressive consumer culture

  • 1.    Expansion of Global Brands - Multinational corporations standardise consumption patterns. Eg-McDonaldization of food habits

  • 2.    Market-Driven Aspirations - Identity increasingly shaped by consumption choices. Eg- Status linked to ownership of branded gadgets like Apple iphones

  • 3.    Advertising and Media Influence - Eg- Targeted digital ads promoting instant gratification and lifestyle consumption.

144

  • 4.    The spread of "Buy Now, Pay Later" schemes has led to a spike in "impulse buying" among Gen Z, replacing the traditional Indian value of Mitavyaya (frugality).

  • 5.    Cultural Commodification - Traditions converted into market products. Eg- Big Billion Days during Diwali

  • 6.    Rise of "Fast Culture" - Globalization of supply chains allows for the rapid production of cheap goods, encouraging a "use and throw" mentality. Eg- Fast Fashion industry

  • 7.    Urban Lifestyle Homogenisation - Eg- rise of mall culture

  • 8.    Youth-Oriented Consumerism - Eg- Social media influencer culture shaping consumption norms.

  • 9.    Inequality-Driven Consumption Pressure - Aspirational consumption among lower middle class despite income gaps.

  • 10.    The traditional Indian value of Mitha-vyaya (balanced spending) is being replaced by the "You Only Live Once" (YOLO) philosophy due to global individualistic culture.

Counter-Argument- Beyond Aggressive Consumerism

  • 1.    Rise of Sustainable and Ethical Consumption - increasing demand for organic food and eco-friendly products.

  • 2.    Minimalism - globalization has spread the philosophy of "Less is More." Eg- the Scandinavian "Lagom" philosophy

  • 3.    Glocalization (Think Global, Act Local) - Eg- McDonald’s menu in India is beef-free, while Domino’s serves Paneer Tikka Pizza.

  • 4.    Global Export of Culture - Eg- International Yoga Day and the global Ayurveda market have moved Indian traditional knowledge to the center of global wellness.

  • 5.    Reassertion of Values ("Returning to Roots") - urban youth increasingly participates in traditional festivals like Ganesh Chaturthi or Chhath Puja as a mark of identity.

  • 6.    Circular Economy Adoption- focus on "Recycle and Upcycle" business models, countering the throwaway culture.

  • 7.    Global Environmental Activism to hold government and corporate accountable for unsustainable policies and practices. Eg- Fridays for Future movement

  • 8.    Revival of Local Crafts through E-commerce - Platforms like Etsy or India’s Jaypore promote traditional "Slow Fashion" (like Handloom) and ethical consumerism

  • 9.    Globalization has also facilitated Universal Human Rights Advocacy - Eg- global #BlackLivesMatter or #MeToo movements

  • 10.    Flow of Knowledge and Ideas - Globalisation enables access to education, research, and innovation. Eg- wikipedia

Globalization is a Janus-faced phenomenon (Anthony Giddens). Improving regulation, social consciousness, and value choices is needed.

2022 - How is the growth of Tier 2 cities related to the rise of a new middle class with an emphasis on the culture of consumption? (10)

Tier 2 cities are urban centers with a population between 50,000 and 100,000. Tier 2 and Tier 3 cities house over 51% of MSMEs and are witnessing a 20-25% annual growth in organized retail.

Reasons for Growth of Tier 2 Cities

  • 1.    Infrastructure Push- Projects like Bharatmala and PM Gati Shakti. Eg- Lucknow-Kanpur Expressway

  • 2.    Increasing industrialization and urbanization - 37% population living in cities

  • 3.    Regional Air Connectivity under UDAN Scheme increasing accessibility.

  • 4.    IT Decentralization- Companies moving to "Spoke-and-Hub" models in cities like Kochi.

  • 5.    Reverse Migration among skilled professionals due to work from home culture and increasing cost of living in Tier-1 cities

  • 6.    Lower Cost of Operations for startups.

  • 7.    Educational Hubs-expansion of IITs, IIMs, and private universities in cities like Jodhpur and Bhubaneswar.

  • 8.    Smart Cities Mission- Digital and civic upgrades in 100 cities improving "Ease of Living."

145

Linkages between growth of tier-2 cities and the new consumption-oriented middle class

  • 1.    Economic Decentralisation - Movement of industries and services beyond metros has generated stable salaried employment.

  • 2.    Rising Disposable Incomes due to rising incomes and lower cost of living allows higher discretionary spending.

  • 3.    Real Estate Boom - Growth of housing, malls, multiplexes, and gated communities reshape lifestyles.

  • 4.    Expansion of organised retail, food chains, and lifestyle services cater to aspirational consumers.

Eg- Zudio, McDonald in tier-2 cities

  • 5.    E-commerce Penetration- Deep logistics networks by Amazon and Flipkart

  • 6.    Digital Penetration - Internet, smartphones, and OTT platforms diffuse global consumer culture. Eg-Online shopping and influencer-driven consumption.

  • 7.    Cultural Shift from Necessity to Lifestyle Consumption - Travel, dining out, fitness centres, and entertainment becoming routine expenses. Eg- fitness chains like Cult.fit

  • 8.    Credit Expansion - Easy access to loans and EMIs fuels consumption. Eg- Buy Now, Pay Later option.

  • 9.    Changing Social Values - Individualism and aspiration replace frugality and traditional restraint.

Ensuring that consumption-led growth is inclusive and sustainable is needed to make Tier-2 cities as engines of equitable urban development.

2021 - What is Cryptocurrency? How does it affect global society? Has it been affecting Indian society also?(15)

Cryptocurrency is a digital or virtual currency designed to function as a medium of exchange. Unlike traditional money , it does not exist in physical form and is not issued by the central bank.

Fact File

  • 1.    Cryptocurrency market size is valued to increase $ 39.75 billion, at a CAGR of 16.7% by 2029.

  • 2.    India cryptocurrency market size was valued at USD 3.0 Billion in 2025

Key Features of Cryptocurrency

  • 1.    Decentralization- Operates on a peer-to-peer network, no need for intermediaries like banks.

  • 2.    Blockchain Technology- A distributed ledger that records all transactions

  • 3.    Cryptography- Uses advanced mathematical protocols to secure transactions.

  • 4.    Immutability- Once a transaction is recorded on the blockchain, it cannot be altered or deleted.

  • 5.    Limited Supply- Most cryptos (like Bitcoin) have a mathematical cap on their total supply.

  • 6.    Pseudonymity- Transactions are tied to digital "wallet addresses" rather than personal identities.

  • 7.    Global Accessibility- Can be accessed by anyone with an internet connection

  • 8.    Eg- Bitcoin, Ethereum, stablecoins, and central-bank-linked digital assets (CBDCs as a response).

Positive Impacts

  • 1.    Financial Inclusion- Provides banking services to the "unbanked" in developing nations.

  • 2.    Lower Remittance Costs- Enables migrants to send money home with negligible fees compared to traditional services like Western Union.

  • 3.    Hedge Against Inflation- Citizens in economies with hyperinflation (e.g., Venezuela, Argentina) use stablecoins to preserve their wealth.

  • 4.    Innovation in Digital Economy - Growth of DeFi, NFTs, and Web3.

  • 5.    Portfolio diversification for investors. Eg- crypto trading apps like Binance

  • 6.    Transparency and Traceability - Public blockchains enable transaction audits.

  • 7.    Tech Innovation and Employment - Eg- Bengaluru has solidified its position as a global Web3 Hub

  • 8.    Boost to Digital Literacy - Awareness of encryption, wallets, and cyber hygiene.

  • 9.    Catalyst for Regulatory Innovation - Eg- Introduction of Reserve Bank of India’s Digital Rupee (CBDC).

  • 10.    Institutional Efficiency- Major global banks (e.g., JP Morgan) now use blockchain for near-instant cross-border settlements.

146

Negative Impacts

  • 1.    Extreme Volatility- Sudden price crashes can lead to catastrophic financial losses for uninformed retail investors. Eg- 2022 Terra-Luna collapse

  • 2.    Environmental Concerns- "Proof of Work" mining consumes massive electricity. Eg- Bitcoin mining consumes more energy than mid-sized countries like Norway.

  • 3.    Illicit Activities- Used by cybercriminals for money laundering, terror funding, and ransomware payments.

  • 4.    Lack of uniform global laws leads to "regulatory arbitrage." Eg- FTX exchange collapse revealing massive gaps in jurisdictional oversight.

  • 5.    Security Risks- Sophisticated hacking of exchanges has resulted in the theft of millions in user funds. Eg- 2024 WazirX breach

  • 6.    Psychological Stress- The 24/7 nature of the market has led to "crypto-addiction" and increased anxiety among younger Gen-Z investors.

  • 7.    Speculative Bubble Behaviour - Prices driven by hype rather than fundamentals. Eg- Sharp rises and falls in meme-based tokens.

  • 8.    Digital Divide - Benefits skewed towards urban, tech-savvy populations.

  • 9.    Widespread use of private crypto can weaken a central bank's control over inflation.

The way forward lies in balanced regulation, public awareness, and responsible integration, ensuring that innovation serves economic development and social welfare, not unchecked speculation.

2022 - Elucidate the relationship between globalization and new technology in a world of scarce resources, with special reference to India. (15)

Thomas Friedman defines globalization as the inexorable integration of markets, nation-states, and technologies, making it farther, faster, deeper, and cheaper. It has accelerated the diffusion of technology thereby enabling efficient use, substitution, and management of scarce resources.

Relationship between globalisation and new technology

  • 1.    New technologies reduce the constraints of distance, time, and cost.

  • 2.    Efficient Resource Utilisation- Eg- Precision agriculture using satellite data and sensors to reduce water and fertiliser use.

  • 3.    Shift from Resource-Intensive to Knowledge-Intensive Growth- Eg- India’s IT-ITeS sector generating exports with minimal material input compared to manufacturing.

  • 4.    Global Diffusion of cleaner and resource-efficient technologies. Eg- Solar and wind technologies.

  • 5.    Integration into Global Value Chains (GVCs) - Technology enables countries to specialise in specific stages of production. Eg- India as the pharmacy of the world.

  • 6.    Resource constraints stimulate technological innovation for low-cost solutions for energy, healthcare, and mobility. Eg- telemedicine under eSanjeevani

  • 7.    The Circular Economy- Globalization facilitates the trade of "waste-as-resource" across borders.

  • 8.    Exposure to global markets compels efficiency and innovation. Eg- Indian manufacturing adopting automation and Industry 4.0 to remain competitive.

  • 9.    Technology for Resource Governance- GIS, remote sensing, and digital dashboards used for water management and climate action in India.

  • 10.    Collaborative R&D- globalization pools global intellect. Eg- ITER project for nuclear fusion involving the EU, US, India, and China.

Negative Impacts

  • 1.    Unsustainable Extraction of mineral resources- Eg- Massive Lithium mining in South America to fuel the global EV boom.

  • 2.    E-Waste Accumulation- Eg- Humanity produces 62 million tonnes of electronic waste every year.

  • 3.    Technological Imperialism- Eg- Disputes over "Intellectual Property Rights" (IPR) for high-efficiency carbon-capture tech.

  • 4.    Carbon Footprint of Logistics- Eg- global shipping and aviation account for ~10% of CO2 emissions.

147

  • 5.    Digital Divide- Access to tech itself becomes a "scarce resource." Eg- Only 40% of the Global South having access to 5G.

  • 6.    Global competition for resources leading to conflicts

  • a.    China’s weaponization of rare earth supply chains.

  • b.    US attempt to take over Greenland

  • 7.    Obsolescence by Design- Tech firms globally produce short-lived products to drive sales, wasting minerals. Eg- Smartphones designed with non-replaceable batteries.

  • 8.    Labor Exploitation for Resources- Eg- Child labor in Cobalt mines in the DRC.

Impact on India

Positive

Negative

Financial inclusion through JAM trinity and UPI has saved over 3.5 lakh cr of government

Richest 1% control more than 40% of total wealth (Oxfam Report)

Renewable Energy Leadership - 3rd in solar and 4th in wind

Technological Dependency- Eg- 70% of semiconductor and EV battery components imported from China.

Service led growth - 55% share in GDP

E-Waste Crisis- India is the world’s third-largest producer

Technology for agriculture. Eg- e-Nam, micro-irrigation

High cost and land fragmentation limits adoption

Start-up Ecosystem - 3rd largest hub for unicorns (125+)

Brain Drain- India’s best tech minds moving to global hubs (Silicon Valley).

Reduced Logistics Costs due to digital supply chains

Overconsumption Culture- Eg- The rise of "Fast Fashion" in Tier-2 cities

Global partnerships for energy security. Eg-Mineral Security Partnership (MSP)

Limited technology transfer from developed countries

For India, the path forward lies in "Frugal Innovation" to ensure that the digital revolution does not come at the cost of the environment.

2023 - Child cuddling is now being replaced by mobile phones. Discuss its impact on the socialization of children. (10)

Socialization is the process through which children internalize values, norms, emotions, and social skills.

Increasing substitution of physical affection with mobile phones is reshaping this process.

Impact on the Socialization of Children

Negative Impacts

  • 1.    Excessive digital mediation risks creating “emotionally under-nurtured but digitally over-stimulated” children.

  • 2.    Weakening of Emotional Bonding - Reduced physical affection hampers secure attachment formation.

  • 3.    Delayed Emotional Development as screens cannot reciprocate emotions. Eg- Children struggle to recognise empathy, affection, and emotional cues.

  • 4.    Impaired Language and Communication Skills due to limited verbal interaction.

148

  • 5.    Excessive screen use affects self-regulation. Eg- Short attention span and impulsive behaviour.

  • 6.    Erosion of Family Interaction - Eg- Children engaged with phones during meals instead of conversation.

  • 7.    Reduced Moral and Value Transmission - Informal teaching through interaction declines. Eg- absence of grand parents in nuclear families

  • 8.    Social Isolation - Digital engagement replaces real-world relationships.

  • 9.    Early Consumerist Socialization - Eg- Desire for gadgets and branded products influenced by online media.

  • 10.    Decline in physical activities lead to health issues. Eg- Roughly 188 million children (1 in 10) are living with obesity in 2025 (UNICEF)

Positive Impacts

  • 1.    Early Cognitive Stimulation - Eg- Storytelling and alphabet-learning apps supporting early literacy.

  • 2.    Digital Social Skills - Familiarity with digital interaction norms prepares children for digital future. Eg-emojis

  • 3.    Bridging Physical Distance and maintaining emotional bonds with distant family. Eg- Video calls with grandparents.

  • 4.    Encourages curiosity and self-directed learning. Eg- Interactive games requiring problem-solving.

  • 5.    Cultural Awareness- Apps expose children to diverse languages and social norms. Eg- learning about Indian history through an educational YouTube series.

Healthy socialization requires balanced parenting, where human touch and attention remain central, and screens are used sparingly and purposefully.

2024 - Globalization has increased urban migration by skilled, young, unmarried women from various classes. How has this trend impacted upon their personal freedom and relationship with family? (15)

Globalization has expanded education, service-sector employment, and transnational labor markets, leading to rising urban migration of women across classes.

Positive impact of urban migration

On personal freedom

  • 1.    Economic autonomy - Regular wages enhance bargaining power over life choices. Eg- A BPO employee independently financing postgraduate studies.

  • 2.    Career choice - Exposure to diversified jobs weakens caste-gender occupational lock-ins.

  • 3.    Education opportunities. Eg- 43% STEM graduates are women

  • 4.    Lifestyle agency - Control over dress, leisure, and daily routines expands self-expression. Eg- late shifts, mixed-gender workplaces.

  • 5.    Delay in marriage - Eg- avg marriage age for educated urban women is 23 compared to 17.5 for uneducated women (NFHS-5).

  • 6.    Sexual and relational choice - Eg- Dating apps facilitating consent-based relationships.

  • 7.    Identity formation - Interaction with diverse, global peers fosters a sense of self-identity and feminist consciousness. Eg- participation in #Metoo Movement

On relationship with family

  • 1.    Status uplift - Family prestige rises with urban employment. Eg- “IT daughter” enhancing matrimonial standing.

  • 2.    Reduced patriarchal control - Eg- Fewer restrictions on mobility and friendships.

  • 3.    Increased Decision-Making Power- Eg- A daughter deciding the purchase of a family asset or funding a sibling’s education.

  • 4.    Migration allows daughters to become the primary caregivers for aging parents, challenging the "son-centric" dependency norm.

149

  • 5.    Inspirational Role Model- encourage younger female relatives to pursue higher education.

  • 6.    Intergenerational learning - New ideas on health, finance, and law flow back home. Eg- Teaching parents digital banking.

Negative impact of urban migration

On personal freedom

  • 1.    Double burden - anxiety regarding urban safety and workplace harassment. Eg- Nirbhaya case

  • 2.    Moral policing - Surveillance shifts from family to landlords/employers.

  • 3.    Lack of a familial support system can lead to severe loneliness and mental health struggles. Eg- rising suicide rates among young women

  • 4.    Cyber-Harassment- Eg- Professional women facing "doxing" or harassment on social media platforms.

  • 5.    Commoditization of Identity- Pressure to conform to "global urban" beauty and fashion standards. Eg- Excessive spending on cosmetics and branded goods to "fit in" at the corporate level.

On relationship with family

  • 1.    Emotional distance - Eg- Festivals spent away from home.

  • 2.    Value Clashes- Divergence in lifestyle creates frequent inter-generational conflict. Eg- Resistance to inter-caste partnerships.

  • 3.    Marriage anxieties - Fear of “urban influence” triggers pressure. Eg- Early matchmaking attempts.

  • 4.    Burnout from Expectations- pressure to be a "perfect professional" in the city and a "traditional daughter" at home leads to high stress.

As argued by Saskia Sassen, global cities generate opportunities alongside precarity. Policy supports like safe housing, urban transport security, labor protections, and family-friendly work norms are essential for realising “Nari Shakti”

2025 - How do you account for the growing fast food industries given that there are increased health concerns in modern society? Illustrate your answer with the Indian experience. (15)

Despite growing health consciousness, non-communicable diseases, and awareness about junk food risks, the fast food industry continues to expand rapidly @ CAGR of 7.3% in India.

Increased health concerns in modern society

  • 1.    Rising obesity levels - NFHS-5 reports overweight/obesity at 24% in women and 23% in men.

  • 2.    Diabetes epidemic - India hosts over 101 million diabetics (ICMR-2023).

  • 3.    Cardiovascular diseases due to High salt and trans-fat consumption from fast foods.

  • 4.    Rising lifestyle disorders among youth - Early onset hypertension and fatty liver disease.

  • 5.    "Hidden Hunger" concern - foods high in energy but deficient in micronutrients like Zinc, Iron, and Vitamin B12.

  • 6.    Public health campaigns - Eg- Fit India Movement and Eat Right India by FSSAI.

Despite this, the fast food industry is growing. Reasons behind it are as follows

  • 1.    Time poverty in urban life - Long work hours reduce cooking time. Eg- Dual-income households in metros.

  • 2.    Changing work patterns - Night shifts favor ready food. Eg- BPO employees ordering late-night meals.

  • 3.    Youth bulge - Large young population (median age is 28) with experimental tastes.

  • 4.    Aspirational consumption - Fast food symbolizes modern, global lifestyle in tier 2 and tier 3 cities

  • 5.    Affordability due to economies of scale - Cheaper than healthy alternatives.

  • 6.    Localization of menus - Cultural adaptation increases acceptability. Eg- McAloo Tikki, paneer pizzas.

  • 7.    Digital food delivery platforms like Zomato and Swiggy reduce transaction cost and effort.

  • 8.    Aggressive marketing and discounts - Price nudges override health logic. Eg- Buy-one-get-one

offers.

150

  • 9.    Street food as indigenous fast food. Eg- Vada pav, momos, chowmein stalls.

  • 10.    Awareness-action gap - Knowledge does not ensure behavior change.

  • 11.    Food as leisure and socialization - Eg- Family outings to QSRs on weekends.

  • 12.    Weak regulatory deterrence - Eg- Calorie labeling not strictly enforced.

Way Forward

  • 1.    Clear front-of-pack labeling - Traffic-light nutrition labels. Eg- UK’s color-coded food labels.

  • 2.    Fiscal nudges - Eg- Mexico’s sugar tax reducing soda consumption.

  • 3.    Restrict junk food near schools. Eg- Chile’s ban on junk food advertising to children.

  • 4.    Strengthening regulation - Eg- FSSAI’s Eat Right certification.

  • 5.    Promoting low-AGE Diet (Advanced Glycation End product) consisting of fruits, vegetables, whole grains, and low-fat dairy products.

Systemic regulation, affordable healthy alternatives, and environments where the healthy choice becomes the easy choice are essential for “Healthy India”

151

Role of Women and Women’s Organization

2018 - ‘Women’s movement in India has not addressed the issues of women of lower social strata.’ Substantiate your view. (15)

"I measure the progress of a community by the degree of progress which women have achieved." - Dr. B.R. Ambedkar

Major women movements in India

  • 1.    Anti-Sati Movement led by Raja Ram Mohan Roy.

  • 2.    Satyashodhak Samaj led by Savitribai and Jyotirao Phule pioneered female education

  • 3.    Anti-Dowry Campaigns (1980s)- Led by groups like Mahila Dakshata Samiti

  • 4.    Women’s Indian Association first to demand women’s suffrage

  • 5.    SEWA - Founded by Ela Bhatt, organized women in SHG and trade unions

  • 6.    Mathura Rape Case Protest (1979-80)- led to first major amendment in Rape Laws

  • 7.    Triple Talaq Movement led by the Bharatiya Muslim Mahila Andolan (BMMA)

  • 8.    Chipko Movement- Led by women like Gaura Devi

  • 9.    Navdanya Movement (Vandana Shiva) - empowers women to preserve biodiversity and seeds

  • 10.    #MeToo Movement - against sexual harassment at workplace

However, they failed to address the issues of women of lower strata

  • 1.    It failed to recognise the triple burden of discrimination (intersection of Caste, Class, and Gender) as it treated "Woman" as a monolithic category. Eg- Hatras rape case

  • 2.    Intersection of gender and religious marginalization was weakly addressed. Eg- Muslim women’s socio-economic exclusion received less attention beyond advocacy of UCC and Triple Talaq

  • 3.    Leadership and agenda dominated by elite women - Early focus on legal reforms like dowry and workplace equality over survival issues like hunger and wage security.

  • 4.    Limited focus on informal and unpaid work - Eg- more focuses on "glass ceilings" in corporate offices, neglecting agricultural laborers. (Over 90% of Dalit women work in the unorganized sector.)

  • 5.    Urban bias in issues and mobilization - Eg- Rural women’s access to drinking water, fuelwood, and sanitation remained peripheral.

  • 6.    Inadequate engagement with tribal women’s concerns - Development-induced displacement received limited feminist mobilization.

  • 7.    Neglect of women issues in conflict zones - Eg- Women in Kashmir and the Northeast did not become sustained focal points of mainstream movements.

  • 8.    Dependence on NGO-ization and donor agendas - Project-driven advocacy replaced mass mobilization among poor women.

  • 9.    Educational Exclusion- Elite feminism focuses on "STEM representation," while lower-strata women struggle with basic literacy and school dropouts.

  • 10.    Health Disparities- feminist debates revolve around "reproductive choice" (abortion rights), while marginalized women face lack of basic maternal healthcare.

  • 11.    Language Barrier- Feminist discourse is largely in English, creating a "vocabulary of exclusion" for rural, non-English speaking women.

  • 12.    Digital Divide- "Hashtag Activism" (#MeToo) failed to reach rural women due to digital illiteracy.

Counter-Argument- Positive Impacts on the Lower Strata

  • 1.    Grassroots Political Entry- Eg- women representation in PRIs stands at 46%

  • 2.    SHGs- NABARD-led SHG movement has empowered millions of rural women through micro-credit and financial literacy. Eg- Kudumbshree in Kerala

  • 3.    Legal empowerment-

  • a.    SC/ST Prevention of Atrocities Act.

  • b.    Right to temple entry after Sabrimala Verdict

  • c.    Triple Talaq ban

  • 4.    Anti-Liquor Movements led by poor women to save household income and reduce domestic abuse.

152

Eg- Arrack Movement in Nellore, Andhra Pradesh

  • 5.    Eco-Feminism- Chipko Movement was essentially a struggle by rural women for their daily fuel and fodder resources.

  • 6.    Labor Reforms- SEWA model has successfully unionized thousands of street vendors and ragpickers.

  • 7.    Dalit Feminism- rise of autonomous groups like the National Federation of Dalit Women (NFDW).

Way Forward

  • 1.    Adopting an "Intersectionality Lens" that acknowledges caste and class alongside gender in every policy.

  • 2.    Caste-Gender Sensitivity Training for police and judiciary

  • 3.    Mainstreaming Gender Budgeting

  • 4.    De-NGO-ization - Revive grassroots feminist politics.

Only by embracing a "Bahujan Feminism" can the movement fulfill Ambedkar’s vision of a truly progressive and equal society.

2019 - What are the continued challenges for women in India against time and space? (15)

Despite constitutional guarantees, legal reforms, and social movements, women in India continue to face persistent challenges that transcend historical periods and vary across geographies and social locations.

Challenges across time

  • 1.    Patriarchal social norms - Enduring preference for male authority and control over women. Eg- son meta preference

  • 2.    Control over female sexuality - Moral policing, honour-based violence, restrictions on marriage choices.

  • a.    Child Marriages - 23% (NFHS-5)

  • b.    Khap panchayats restricting wearing jeans etc

  • 3.    Unequal care burden - women spend an average of 305 minutes daily on unpaid domestic work, compared to only 98 minutes for men.

  • 4.    Educational discontinuity due to marriage and caregiving. Eg- Higher dropout rates among adolescent girls.

  • 5.    Economic marginalization

  • a.    Women constitute 63% of agriculture workforce but own only 13% of land

  • b.    Low FLFPR - 41% in India vs 71% in China

  • 6.    Violence against women - domestic violence, workplace harassment etc. Eg- Nirbhaya Case

  • 7.    Legal-social gap - Eg- proxy representation in PRIs (Sarpanch Pati syndrome)

  • 8.    Challenges across life-cycle stages

  • 1.    Before Birth - female foeticide

  • 2.    Childhood and adolescent stage - Nutrition neglect (57% anemia), high dropouts

  • 3.    Adulthood - Maternal health and autonomy issues. Eg- early pregnancies.

  • 4.    Elderly women’s vulnerability - Loneliness, poverty, and lack of social security

Challenges across space

  • 1.    Rural-urban divide

  • a.    Limited mobility in rural areas due to safety and norms.

  • b.    Safety concerns and informal employment in cities.

  • 2.    Caste and community-based challenges

  • a.    Caste-based violence - Gender violence used as social control. Eg- Hatras rape case

  • b.    Mining and infrastructure-induced displacement of Tribal women

  • c.    Minority women’s double marginalization - Eg- Muslim women’s low labour force participation.

  • 3.    Challenges in Economic space

  • a.    Concentration in low paying care and domestic work

  • b.    Glass ceiling effect - discrimination and low representation at higher positions

153

  • 4.    Digital Divide - 51.6% of women aged 15 years and above in rural areas do not own a mobile phone (NSO)

  • 5.    Regional and cultural disparities

  • a.    Regional inequality - Eg- Higher TFR in Bihar (3.0 vs national average of 1.9)

  • b.    Violence in conflict zones - Eg- gang-rape and naked parade of two Kuki-Zo women in Manipur

Improvements made

  • 1.    Political

  • a.    Nari Shakti Vandan Adhiniyam

  • b.    46% representation in PRIs

  • 2.    Legal

  • a.    stringent and gender-sensitive provisions in Bhartiya Nyaya Sanhita

  • b.    PCPNDT and POSH Acts

  • 3.    Health - Institutional Deliveries increased from 78.9% (NFHS-4) to 88.6% (NFHS-5).

  • 4.    Education- women constitute 43% of STEM graduates.

  • 5.    Economic- Over 28 crore women now have personal Jan Dhan accounts.

  • 6.    Asset Ownership- Under PM Awas Yojana, 70% of houses are registered in the name of women.

Way Forward

  • 1.    Mainstreaming gender budgeting

  • 2.    Expand One Stop Centres (OSCs) under Mission Shakti for integrated support services

  • 3.    Introduce gender sensitisation modules in school curricula nationwide.

  • 4.    Comprehensive Healthcare

  • a.    Expand coverage and financial incentives under Janani Suraksha Yojana and PM Matru Vandana Yojana

  • b.    Leverage digital health tools like MP’s SUMAN SAKHI chatbot to provide real-time support to expecting mothers.

  • 5.    Women-Centric Urban Infrastructure- Eg- “Sakhi Niwas” hostels for working women

Thus, sustainable gender justice requires simultaneous expansion of resources, agency, and participation.

2021 - Examine the role of ‘Gig Economy’ in the process of empowerment of women in India. (10)

Gig Economy refers to a labor market characterized by the prevalence of short-term contracts or freelance work as opposed to permanent jobs.

Fact File

  • 1.    Total gig workforce in India is 1.2 crore, a 55% increase since 2021

  • 2.    Women comprise over 28% of the total gig workforce

Role of Gig Economy in Women Empowerment

  • 1.    Work-Life Integration- Gig work offers flexible hours, allowing women to balance the "Double Burden" (41% of Indian women are primary caregivers).

  • 2.    Economic Autonomy- It provides a direct source of income for women in Tier-II and Tier-III cities where formal job opportunities are scarce.

  • 3.    Formalizing Informal Skills- Platforms like Urban Company have professionalized domestic skills. Eg-beauticians, cleaning.

  • 4.    Bypassing Social Gatekeepers- Digital platforms allow women to access work directly, reducing dependence on male family members.

  • 5.    Financial Inclusion- Gig platforms mandate digital payments into personal bank accounts, enabling women to build a "credit history" for small business loans.

  • 6.    Safety through Technology- Features like real-time GPS tracking, SOS buttons provide a sense of security for women working in transit-heavy roles. Eg- zomato delivery

154

  • 7.    Upskilling Opportunities- Many platforms provide vocational training.

  • 8.    Re-entry into Workforce- Gig work provides a low-barrier entry point for women returning from "maternity breaks".

  • 9.    Global Market Access- High-skill gig workers serve international clients via platforms like Upwork or Fiverr.

  • 10.    Collective Bargaining- rise of unions like the Gig and Platform Services Workers Union (GIPSWU). Eg- Diwali ‘digital strike’ by female gig workers in 2024

Challenges for Women in the Gig Economy

  • 1.    Digital Divide- 51.6% of women aged 15 years and above in rural areas do not own a mobile phone (NSO)

  • 2.    Lack of Social Security- Eg- excluded from the Maternity Benefit Act and paid sick leaves.

  • 3.    Wage Disparity- 40% of gig workers earn below ₹15,000/month, and women often earn less due to an inability to work "high-incentive" late-night hours.

  • 4.    Occupational Segregation- women confined to beauty and care while men dominate higher-paying delivery and ride-hailing sectors.

  • 5.    Safety Risks- Women in home-service gigs face risks of harassment and customer misconduct

  • 6.    Gig platforms classify women as ‘independent contractors’ - excluded from protections under POSH Act

Way Forward

  • 1.    Adopting global best practices - Eg- Platform Work Directive (EU)

  • 2.    Skill Diversification- Government-led missions to train women in sectors like EV-cab driving and tech-maintenance to break occupational silos.

  • 3.    Algorithmic Accountability- Transparency and fairness in digital management systems that control job assignments, worker ratings, and payouts.

A multi-stakeholder approach is necessary to turn the gig economy from a place of exploitation to one of opportunity for women.

2023 - Explain why suicide among young women is increasing in Indian Society. (10)

India accounts for approximately 37% of global female suicide deaths. It is now the leading cause of death for Indian women aged 15-39.

Causes Behind Increasing Suicide Among Young Women

  • 1.    Housewives - over 50% of suicides

  • a.    Marriage-related pressures and domestic violence

  • b.    social isolation, lack of economic agency, and the "unseen" nature of domestic labor

  • 2.    Students

  • a.    Academic and career stress - Rising aspirations without sufficient support. Eg- Student suicides in Kota.

  • b.    Inadequate counselling facilities in schools, colleges, and hostels.

  • 3.    Working women

  • a.    Cyberbullying and Online harassment culture affect self-worth. Eg- Trolling, non-consensual sharing of images.

  • b.    Workplace discrimination and sexual harassment

  • 4.    Social causes

  • a.    Intersectional vulnerabilities - Caste, class, and rural disadvantage intensify distress. Eg-Dalit and rural women

  • b.    Patriarchal control - Restrictions on mobility, education, and choice generate helplessness. Eg- Family opposition to inter-caste or inter-faith relationships.

  • c.    Child Marriages - 23% women are married before 18 (NFHS-5) - exposes them to abusive relationship

  • 5.    Weak institutional support systems -

  • a.    Mental health neglect and stigma - Depression and anxiety often go undiagnosed and untreated.

  • b.    India has 0.75 psychiatrist per 1 lakh people (global avg - 13)

155

Way Forward

  • 1.    Implementation of National Suicide Prevention Strategy to reduce suicide mortality by 10% by 2030.

  • 2.    WHO’s "LIVE LIFE" Approach- limiting access to means , responsible media reporting to prevent "copycat" suicides, and fostering life skills in adolescents.

  • 3.    Training "frontline" community members like ASHA workers to identify early warning signs and providing counselling.

  • 4.    Implementing Saha Guidelines for preventing student suicide

  • 5.    Integrating "Cyber-Safety" and mental health resilience into the school curriculum.

  • 6.    Workplace Wellness Mandates- Eg- POSH workshops and mental health support

As highlighted by World Health Organization, suicide is preventable through timely intervention and supportive environments.

2024 - Distinguish between gender equality, gender equity and women’s empowerment. Why is it important to take gender concerns into account in programme design and implementation? (10)

"I measure the progress of a community by the degree of progress which women have achieved." - Dr. B.R. Ambedkar

Gender Equality

Gender Equity

Women’s Empowerment

Equal rights, responsibilities, and opportunities for all.

Fairness and justice in the distribution of benefits and burdens.

The process of gaining agency and power to make life choices.

Assumes everyone starts from the same level.

Recognizes that people have different starting points.

Recognizes that the "powerless" need tools to claim their rights.

Focuses on Formal Rights (Same for all).

Focuses on Substantive Justice (Fair for all).

Focuses on Agency & Capacity (Power to act).

Providing the same resources to everyone.

Affirmative Action.

Economic and political voice.

equal pay for equal work, equal voting rights

Nari Shakti Adhiniyam, 2023

Stand Up India scheme, SHG-Bank linkage program

Need to take Gender Concerns into account

  • 1.    Better targeting in welfare schemes - Eg- women constitute 63% of agriculture workforce but own only 13% land - incorporating gender concerns can address this gap

  • 2.    Correcting historical disadvantage - Eg- houses registered in the name of women under PM Awas Yojana

  • 3.    Maximizing Economic Returns- The IMF notes that closing the gender gap could boost India's GDP by 27%.

  • 4.    Improving Efficiency- Eg- involving women in water committees under Jal Jeevan Mission ensures better maintenance as they are the primary water managers.

  • 5.    Upholding Constitutional Mandates- Article 15(3) allows for special provisions for women. Gender-sensitive design fulfills this obligation.

  • 6.    Long term Social Change happens when women are "agents" rather than "beneficiaries." Eg-Kudumbashree in Kerala

  • 7.    Achieving SDG’s like 1(ending poverty), 3 (health and wellbeing) and 5 (gender equality)

  • 8.    Enhancing development outcomes - Gender inclusion multiplies benefits. Eg- Educating girls

156

improves health, nutrition, and intergenerational outcomes.

  • 9.    Global best practices and commitments - Eg- Gender budgeting advocated by UN Women.

To awaken the people, it is the women who must be awakened. Once she is on the move, the family moves, the village moves, the nation moves.

Communalism, Regionalism & Secularism.

2018 - How is the Indian concept of secularism different from the western model of secularism? Discuss. (10)

Secularism is a political and constitutional principle that governs the relationship between the state and religion, ensuring that governance operates independently of theological control.

Similarities and Common Foundations

  • 1.    Rejection of Theocracy

  • 2.    Equality Before Law

  • 3.    Non-Discrimination Mandate

  • 4.    Freedom of Individual Conscience

  • 5.    Rational Civic Ordering

  • 6.    Peaceful Coexistence

Difference Between Indian and Western Secularism

Western Model (Strict Separation)

Indian Model (Principled Distance)

Mutual Exclusion: Total wall of separation between church and state. Eg: French Laïcité.

Sarva Dharma Sambhava: Equal respect and tolerance for all religious faiths. Eg: Article 25.

Complete Non-Intervention: The state cannot interfere in religious affairs at all.

Principled Intervention: State can intervene to fix internal social evils. Eg: Untouchability ban.

Individual-Centric: Focuses purely on individual rights, ignoring community metrics. Eg: French turban ban.

Community-Centric: Specifically protects cultural and educational rights of minorities. Eg: Article 30.

Zero State Funding: No public taxes can ever be given to religious bodies.

Equidistant Support: State funds all religious institutions fairly without bias. Eg: Haj and Mansarovar subsidies.

Negative Liberty: Protects citizens strictly from state-enforced religious diktats. Eg: Secular civil oaths.

Positive Liberty: Actively assists citizens in practicing their personal faith freely. Eg: Sikh Kirpan exemptions.

Absolute Uniformity: One uniform civil law applies to all citizens universally. Eg: American Civil Code.

Plural Personal Laws: Accommodates distinct religious marriage and inheritance customs. Eg: Hindu/Muslim personal laws.

157

Ban on Public Symbolism: Strict ban on religious attire or symbols in state structures. Eg: French school hijab ban.

Inclusive Pluralism: State functions formally display diverse multi-faith elements. Eg: Multi-faith prayer launches.

Internal Reform Mechanism: Religious groups must drive their own reforms without courts.

Judicial Reform Engine: State courts systematically strike down oppressive religious practices. Eg: Sabarimala verdict.

Mono-Religious Context: Evolved out of friction within a single dominant faith. Eg: Catholic vs Protestant.

Multi-Religious Context: Designed to manage ancient, deeply rooted multi-faith setups. Eg: Hindu-Muslim-Sikh-Christian dynamics.

While the Western model enforces a strict, rigid wall of separation between state and religion, the Indian concept maintains a dynamic, principled distance, allowing the state to balance deep religious pluralism with necessary social reform.

2019 - What are the challenges to our cultural practices in the name of secularism? (10)

Secularism in the Indian context implies a “principled distance”, ensuring freedom of conscience while upholding constitutional morality, equality, and public order.

Challenges to cultural practices in the name of secularism

  • 1.    State intervention in religious institutions dilutes traditional autonomy. Eg- Government management of Hindu temples

  • 2.    Courts determining “essential practices” (Shirur Math case) oversimplify lived culture.

  • a.    Sabrimala temple verdict

  • b.    Triple talaq ban

  • 3.    Uniformity over diversity - Secular frameworks ignore internal plurality within religions.

  • 4.    Marginalization of indigenous and folk traditions - Eg- ban on Jallikattu or kambala

  • 5.    UCC can lead to erosion of distinct cultural identity of tribals and the "Legal Pluralism" guaranteed under Article 25.

  • 6.    Environmental Regulations on Festivals restrict cultural expressions.

  • a.    Supreme Court's ban on firecrackers during Diwali,

  • b.    Restrictions on loudspeakers for Azaan/Bhajans

  • c.    Height limits for Dahi Handi pyramids.

  • 7.    Politicization of secularism for vote bank politics. Eg- controversy over Hijab ban in schools in Karnataka

  • 8.    Intervention in Food Culture- Eg- meat-sale bans during religious festivals like Paryushan

  • 9.    Right to profess and propagate religion is misused for forced conversions especially of tribals

Way Forward

  • 1.    Substantive secularism - Balance cultural freedom with equality, dignity, and justice.

Judicial restraint - Limit theological interpretation to constitutional necessity.

  • 2.    Protection of pluralism - Safeguard folk, tribal, and minority cultural practices.

  • 3.    Adopting Multicultural Secularism - active accommodation of religious symbols

  • 4.    Promoting Inter-Faith & Intra-Faith Dialogue for “reforms from within” (T.N. Madan)

When practiced as principled neutrality with respect for pluralism, secularism protects both cultural practices and constitutional values.

158

2022 - Are tolerance, assimilation and pluralism the key elements in the making of an Indian form of secularism? Justify your answer. (15)

Secularism in the Indian context implies a “principled distance”, ensuring freedom of conscience while upholding constitutional morality, equality, and public order.

Key Elements of Indian Secularism

Tolerance

  • 1.    Historical coexistence of faiths - Eg- Coexistence of Hinduism, Buddhism, Jainism, Islam, Christianity, Sikhism over centuries.

  • 2.    Constitutional protection - Freedom to profess, practice, and propagate religion under Articles 25 reflects tolerance towards different religious practices

  • 3.    Sarva Dharma Sambhava remains the philosophical bedrock of Indian secularism, ensuring no single religion is prioritized.

  • 4.    Article 51A (f)- Mandates a Fundamental Duty to "value and preserve the rich heritage of our composite culture."

  • 5.    India's history of sheltering persecuted communities (Parsis, Jews, Tibetans) highlights civilizational

tolerance.

Assimilation

  • 1.    Cultural assimilation through syncretism - Eg- Bhakti-Sufi traditions blending devotional practices.

  • 2.    Rejection of forced assimilation - Eg- protection to cultural practices of tribals under 6th schedule

  • 3.    Linguistic Assimilation- Eighth Schedule reflects an assimilation of Sanskrit, and regional dialects.

  • 4.    Architecture and Art- The Indo-Islamic style (Taj Mahal, Red Fort) is a physical manifestation of the assimilation of two distinct cultural worlds.

  • 5.    Only practices violating equality and dignity are reformed. Eg- Abolition of untouchability under Article

17.

Pluralism as the core principle

  • 1.    Recognition of diversity as intrinsic - Eg- Legal recognition of multiple personal laws.

  • 2.    Indian secularism emphasizes principled distance (Rajeev Bhargava) rather than equidistance. Eg-State support for pilgrimages.

  • 3.    Article 29 & 30- Provides the "Right of Minorities" to establish and administer educational institutions, protecting their distinct language, script, or culture.

  • 4.    Religious Denominations (Article 26)- Grants every religious group the right to manage their own affairs in matters of religion.

  • 5.    National holidays on festivals of all religions. Eg- diwali, eid etc

Other Aspects of Indian Secularism

  • 1.    Reformatory Justice- state can intervene in religion to ban harmful practices like Sati, or Triple Talaq.

  • 2.    State Funding to religious schools as long as they meet secular educational standards.

  • 3.    Substantive Equality- It focuses on equality between religious groups as much as equality within them. Practiced as principled neutrality with respect for pluralism, Indian secularism protects both cultural practices and constitutional values.

159

2018 - ‘Communalism arises either due to power struggle or relative deprivation.’ Argue by giving suitable illustrations. (15)

In the Indian context, communalism refers to a political ideology that uses religious identity to mobilize groups for secular ends. As per T.K. Oommen, it is the "politicization of religious identity."


Power Struggle Leading to Communalism

  • 1.    Colonial Legacy of "Divide and Rule"- The British used communalism to prevent the rise of a united nationalist front. Eg-separate electorates under Morley-Minto Reforms.

  • 2.    Partition of India due to power struggle between Muslim League and Hindu Mahasabha.

  • 3.    Electoral Mobilization-

"communal polarization" for vote bank politics. Eg- Ram Janmabhoomi movement

  • 4.    The Institutionalized Riot System - Paul Brass argues that riots are produced through three stages-Preparation (constant tension), Activation, and Explanation for electoral benefits. Eg- hate speech-riots-blame game.

  • 5.    Instrumentalist school- as per Asghar Ali Engineer, it is a tool used by the elites to safeguard their leadership and prevent class-based mobilization of the poor.

  • 6.    Administrative Partisanship- If the state machinery appears biased, it emboldens communal actors. Eg- administrative paralysis during 1984 Anti-Sikh riots.

  • 7.    Politicization of history - Eg- Conflicts over Gyanvapi Mosque or Shahi Idgah serve as sites for asserting cultural and political hegemony.

  • 8.    Separatist movements like Khalistan and insurgency in North-East highlights struggle for political autonomy

  • 9.    Political underrepresentation - Lack of voice in governance intensifies alienation. Eg- only 24 muslim MPs in 18th LS

Relative Deprivation Leading to Communalism

  • 1.    As per Ted Robert Gurr, relative deprivation occurs when there is a perceived gap between a group's "value expectations" and their "value capabilities".

  • 2.    Sir Syed Ahmed Khan advised Muslims not to join congress to increase their representation in government jobs. (Loyal Muhammadans of India - book)

  • 3.    Socio-Economic Backwardness -

  • a.    Sachar Committee Report (2006) highlighted the lack of Muslims in government jobs, fueling a sense of systemic deprivation.

  • b.    Ghettoization and poverty in cities foster "us vs. them" mentalities.

  • 4.    Economic Competition and conflict

  • a.    1961 Jabalpur Riots due to competition between Hindu and Muslim bidi entrepreneurs

  • b.    Conflicts over resources are given a communal color to mobilize support quickly. Eg- Moplah revolt of 1921 by Mapilla muslims against muslim landlords

  • 5.    Majoritarianism- fear among minorities of suppression of their language or traditions by a secular or majority culture. Eg- portest against triple talaq.

  • 6.    Educational Mismatch- illiteracy and unemployment among youth makes them susceptible to communal outfits that offer a sense of identity and purpose. Eg- Burhan Vani

160

” WARNING: This COPY is ENCRYPTED and FOR PERSONAL j USE ONLY. Any unauthorised use will attract legal action.

  • 7.    Migration and fear of demographic change- Native populations feel deprived of jobs by "outsiders." Eg- Communal tensions in Assam due to migration of Bangladeshi muslims

Way Forward

  • 1.    Implementing the Prakash Singh Directives to ensure the police act as an impartial force during tensions

  • 2.    Implementing recommendations of the Amitabh Kundu Committee to ensure development reaches the "bottom-most" layers of all communities.

  • 3.    Civic Integration (Ashutosh Varshney) through mixed-religion residential societies and business associations to act as a buffer against riots.

  • 4.    Regulating Hate Speech- Strict enforcement of MCC during elections.

  • 5.    Community Policing- Scaling models like Mohalla Committees (Mumbai).

  • 6.    Establishing Fast Track Courts to ensure timely persecution of perpetrators of riot

Inclusive development policies and ethical political leadership are needed to ensure that power competition does not fracture social cohesion and deprivation does not become a communal grievance.

Three elements of communalism

  • 1.    A belief that people who follow the same religion have common secular interests i.e. they have the same political, economic and social interests.

  • 2.    A notion that, in a multi-religious society like India, these common secular interests of one religion are dissimilar and divergent from the interests of the followers of another religion.

  • 3.    The interests of the followers of the different religion or of different ‘communities’ are seen to be completely incompatible, antagonist and hostile.

2018 - Discuss whether formation of new states in recent times is beneficial or not for the economy of India. (15)

Formation of Chhattisgarh, Jharkhand, Uttarakhand in 2000, Telangana in 2014, and the reorganization of Jammu and Kashmir in 2019 were driven by demands for administrative efficiency, regional development, identity recognition, and better governance.

Benefits of new state formation for the economy of India

  • 1.    Higher growth rate - Eg- Uttarakhand’s economy has grown over 20 times in size in 23 years

  • 2.    Balanced regional development - Addresses neglect of backward regions within larger states. Eg-Uttarakhand focused on hill infrastructure and tourism-led growth.

  • 3.    Improved capital expenditure prioritization - Eg- Telangana increased irrigation spending through projects like Kaleshwaram.

  • 4.    Fiscal focus on local needs - Eg- Hill-area connectivity in Uttarakhand vs plains-focused spending earlier.

  • 5.    Better resource utilization -

  • a.    Telangana focused on IT and Pharma, leading to a GSDP share increase from 4.1% in 2014 to nearly 5% of national GDP by 2024-25.

  • b.    Jharkhand (40% of India’s mineral wealth) and Chhattisgarh significantly improved their mining and steel output post-formation.

  • 6.    Employment generation - Eg- New state secretariats, universities, and agencies creating jobs.

  • 7.    Competitive federalism gains - States innovate to attract investment. Eg- Telangana’s TS-iPASS single-window clearance system.

  • 8.    Urban growth engines - New capitals stimulate construction and services. Eg- Development of Amravati in Andhra Pradesh.

  • 9.    Administrative efficiency - reduced the "distance" between the government and the governed lead to faster and efficient decision-making. Eg- improved PDS coverage in Chhattisgarh.

161

Counter-arguments - drawbacks for the economy of India

  • 1.    Revenue dependence - New states rely heavily on central transfers.

  • 2.    Uneven development within new states - Eg- Tribal belts in Chhattisgarh remain underdeveloped.

  • 3.    Fragmentation of the national market - Eg- Different state-level compliances increasing transaction costs.

  • 4.    Inter-state disputes over resources - Water, power, and assets become contentious. Eg-Krishna-Godavari water disputes between Telangana and Andhra Pradesh.

  • 5.    High administrative costs -

  • a.    Creation of capitals, assemblies, and cadres strains finances. Eg- creation of Amravati

  • b.    Increase in per-unit costs of administration due to duplication of departments.

  • c.    Over-Administration also fuels corruption. Eg- Pooja Singhal case

  • 6.    Weak institutional capacity - New administrations take time to mature. Eg- Staffing shortages and governance gaps in Uttarakhand.

  • 7.    No automatic growth guarantee - Outcomes depend on governance quality. Eg- political instability in states like Jharkhand and Chhattisgarh deter long-term foreign investment.

  • 8.    Environmental Degradation- Eg- industrialization and urbanization has led to disasters like the 2013 Kedarnath floods and 2023 Joshimath subsidence - High economic losses

Way Forward

  • 1.    Formation 2nd state reorganisation commission as suggested by Sudha Pai

  • 2.    Strengthening cooperative federalism through Inter-state council - mandatory meetings and enforcement powers

  • 3.    Strengthening fiscal federalism - Eg- state representation in Finance commission

New state formation should be guided by objective economic criteria and robust transition planning to ensure that political reorganization strengthens economic growth.

2020 - Do you agree that regionalism in India appears to be a consequence of rising cultural assertiveness? Argue. (10)

Regionalism is political, social, or cultural mobilization around a specific region, rooted in language, culture, history, economy, or perceived neglect, seeking greater autonomy, recognition, or resources.

Regionalism as a Consequence of Rising Cultural Assertiveness

  • 1.    Linguistic assertiveness against cultural centralization- Eg- Anti-Hindi agitations in Tamil Nadu or the recent Kannada-first signage mandates in Karnataka.

  • 2.    Ethno-cultural nationalism - Region imagined as a cultural nation.

  • a.    Tamil cultural nationalism emphasizing classical language and Dravidian heritage.

  • b.    Demand for Greater Nagalim rooted in the assertion of a unique Naga identity.

  • 3.    Religious identity also acts as a base for regional assertiveness. Eg- demand for Khalistan

  • 4.    Preservation of Folk Traditions- Eg- protests against Jallikattu Ban (2017) were framed as an assertion of Tamil "Veeru" (bravery) against "outsider" legal impositions.

  • 5.    Cultural symbolism is used for political mobilization. Eg- renaming Bombay to Mumbai and Calcutta to Kolkata.

  • 6.    Sons of the Soil Doctrine- Movements like the Shiv Sena’s initial "Marathi Manoos" campaign assert that locals must have the first right over the state's resources.

  • 7.    Sub-Regional Assertion- Eg- demand for Tulu Nadu based on the distinct Tulu language and culture.

  • 8.    Resistance to "Mainstreaming"- Tribal regionalism in Jharkhand and Chhattisgarh began as an assertion of Adivasi culture against the "Diku" (outsiders).

Other Factors Driving Regionalism in India

  • 1.    Inter-State Disparities- widening economic gap between the prosperous South/West and the lagging East/North creates friction. Eg- violence against Bihari migrants in Gujarat

  • 2.    Relative Deprivation

  • a.    Vidarbha in Maharashtra.

  • b.    Demand for Telangana before 2014

162

  • 3.    Paul Brass argues that regionalism is politically constructed, where cultural markers are activated by elites for political benefits. Eg- invoking “bengal pride” by TMC

  • 4.    Inter-state disputes over resources fuels regionalism. Eg- Krishna-Godavari water disputes between Telangana and Andhra Pradesh.

  • 5.    Administrative Neglect- Large states become too big to govern, leading to the demand for smaller states. Eg- demand for the division of UP into Harit Pradesh, Purvanchal, Bundelkhand, and Awadh Pradesh

Way Forward

  • 1.    Cooperative Federalism- Strengthening the Inter-State Council (Article 263)

  • 2.    Balanced Regional Development- Eg- aspirational district program

  • 3.    Scaling the "Ek Bharat Shreshtha Bharat" program for promoting fraternity

  • 4.    Administrative Decentralization- empowering Local Bodies

A mature Indian federalism must promote inclusive development, autonomy with unity, and diversity with constitutional fraternity.

2024 - What is regional disparity? How does it differ from diversity? How serious is the issue of regional disparity in India? (15)

Regional disparity refers to the significant imbalance in economic and social development across different geographical regions. It represents the widening inter-state and intra-state gap in terms of per capita income, industrialization, and basic infrastructure.

Reasons Behind Regional Disparity in India

  • 1.    Colonial "Presidency" bias (Bombay, Bengal, Madras).

  • 2.    Geographical Constraints- Landlocked nature of Bihar.

  • 3.    Uneven distribution of resources

  • 4.    Green Revolution Bias- focus limited to Punjab, Haryana, and Western UP.

  • 5.    Infrastructural Gaps- North East India

  • 6.    Varying literacy rates and skill levels (e.g., Kerala vs. Bihar).

7. Political instability in some states deterred long-term investment. Difference Between Regional Disparity and Diversity

Feature

Regional Disparity

Regional Diversity

Nature

Quantitative & Negative imbalance. Eg- Per capita income

Qualitative & Positive variety. Eg-Language

Origin

Result of policy failure or geography.

Result of history, ethnicity, and climate.

Basis

Built on lack of equity.

Built on the richness of identity.

Impact

Leads to migration and social unrest.

Hinders national growth.

Enhances "Composite Culture”.

Promotes “unity in diversity”

Goal

Needs to be minimized or eliminated.

Needs to be preserved and celebrated.

State Interventio n

State acts as a Redistributor.

State acts as a Custodian/Protector.

Indicators

GDP, IMR, Literacy, HHI.

Religion, Language, Dress, Cuisine.

163

Seriousness of Regional Disparity in India

  • 1.    Political disparity

  • a.    80 MPs from UP vs 29 from Kerala and only 2 from Sikkim

  • b.    MP from Bihar represents twice as much people as that of TN

  • 2.    Economic Disparity

  • a.    The per capita income of Goa or Sikkim is nearly 5-7 times higher than that of Bihar

  • b.    Nearly 60% of India’s FDI is concentrated in just three states- Maharashtra, Karnataka, and Gujarat.

  • c.    Infrastructure deficit in poorer regions like North-East, Chhattisgarh, Jharkhand

  • 3.    Health Outcomes- Infant Mortality Rate (IMR) in Kerala (approx. 6) is comparable to developed nations, while in Madhya Pradesh, it is above 40.
